verses.commentary: Some time later. Jacob's illness. Final blessing approaching.
News energizes him. Final effort. Sits up for blessing ceremony. Formal occasion.
Blessed me. Jacob recounts Bethel vision (28:10-22, 35:9-15). God Almighty. The covenant name.
Increase numbers. Abrahamic promises quoted. Fruitfulness. Many nations. Land inheritance.
Joseph's sons elevated to full tribe status. Joseph gets double portion through his sons.
Children after them will be yours. Future children of Joseph stay under Joseph. But these two are independent tribes. Clarifies status of potential other children.
So I buried her there beside the road to Ephrath (that is, Bethlehem). Rachel died. His great sorrow. Her early death.
Who are these? Jacob's failing eyesight mirrors Isaac's (27:1). The pattern repeats. Blessing passes despite physical limitation.
They are the sons God has given me here, Joseph said to his father. They are sons God gave me here. God's gift. Blessing ceremony begins.
Old age. Could hardly see. Like Isaac was blind (27:1). Jacob now blind. Physical affection. Love expressed.
Now God let me see your children too. Jacob's gratitude. Exceeded hopes. God's grace. Seeing grandchildren is bonus blessing.
Humble posture.
Manasseh (firstborn) positioned for right hand blessing. Ephraim (younger) for left hand.
Though younger. Crossing arms. Deliberate cross.

Blessed Joseph. May God before whom Abraham and Isaac walked. God who shepherded me all my life. Blessing addresses Joseph (through his sons). Invokes generational faith. Abraham and Isaac. God as shepherd. Lifetime testimony. Shepherd imagery.
Bless these boys. Increase greatly. Deliverer. Blessing on boys. Multiplication promised.
Displeased. Wrong order. Tries to correct.
Joseph protests. This is wrong. Expects correction.
Father refused. Become great. I know. Not a mistake. Group of nations. Israel (northern) often called Ephraim.
May God make you like Ephraim and Manasseh. Put Ephraim ahead. Blessing formula established.
God will be with you. Jacob's farewell. Death imminent. Promise of divine presence. Return to promised land.
Shechem ridge (Hebrew shekem, shoulder/ridge). Military conquest mentioned (not elsewhere in Genesis).
